summary: When Dan Lewis and Grant Goodale were raising Convoy's early rounds, the single biggest lever wasn't the pitch deck or market story — it was having five or six engineers already committed and working on the product before any money was raised. He put their names, photos, and willingness to serve as references directly into the fundraising narrative, turning a team of volunteers into de facto proof of technical execution. This mattered because early-stage generalist investors can't independently evaluate an idea's technical feasibility, so visible, named engineering commitment substituted for that judgment. It's a specific, replicable fundraising tactic rather than generic "build a great team" advice.
quote: we had five or six people working on this who were engineers and helping us when we wanted to go raise money that was the thing that made all the difference the other stuff I mentioned is super important having the engineering team doing it and committed and like their names on a slide and their pictures and like them willing to be references changed it

summary: Lewis used an explicit investor-pitch framework distinguishing "linear" businesses, where consistent effort and thoughtfulness predictably produce results (e.g. trucking/logistics), from "binary" or scale-dependent businesses that fail entirely unless they hit massive scale (e.g. gaming, social/messaging networks). He deployed this framing deliberately to argue Convoy was a safer, more derisked bet than other opportunities investors were weighing, since most generalist investors couldn't independently judge trucking versus gaming versus social networking. As evidence, he pointed to Path, a social network that reached medium scale but never broke out, arguing binary categories don't really support mid-sized outcomes — you scale or you die. This is a specific mental model he used as fundraising ammunition, not a generic "know your market" statement.
quote: is this a linear business or a nonlinear business is this a binary business or is this a business that has sort of a gradient ... there are a lot of businesses that really aren't going to be successful unless they're massive unless they really nail it like gaming is one of the ones that kind of falls into that department ... whereas there are other businesses that are much more linear and effort and I think they're less risky ... trucking for example and logistics I felt like it was less risky because I felt like we could put the energy in and do the homework ... you don't see a lot of like mid-sized social networks like path right kind of was medium sized and it's done it's done it has to be massive or it doesn't really go

summary: Convoy never considered bootstrapping, and Lewis's reasoning was tied to a precise, narrow window of technological readiness: truck driver smartphone penetration, data plan availability, and GPS accuracy had only recently crossed a threshold that made a trucking marketplace app viable, whereas just a few years earlier — especially in rural areas — that infrastructure simply didn't exist. Believing this created a limited first-mover window against incumbents, he treated speed as directly convertible into money, so raising outside capital was chosen specifically to buy pace rather than for capital's own sake. This is a specific, dated causal chain (device penetration plus data plans plus GPS accuracy hitting a tipping point) rather than a generic "raise money to move fast" claim.
quote: we didn't consider bootstrapping ... couldn't do this the reason is that so we looked at this business as hey technology just shifted truck drivers didn't have smartphones two years ago ... and gps wasn't accurate enough everywhere just even a few years ago right especially in rural areas ... we believe that there was a window of opportunity to go after this industry and we timed it really well timing is a huge part of any startup ... time equals money to accelerate us getting to market

summary: Right after Convoy closed its seed round, investors gave Lewis a specific, non-obvious piece of advice: write the Series B pitch deck immediately, before even fundraising for Series A, on the logic that founders who don't work backward from that future story now "may never" get there. He acted on it literally — after the seed round, the team wrote out what the Series A story needed to say and then reverse-engineered which customer diversity, shipment volume, and territory/team milestones they needed to produce that story, rather than letting traction passively dictate the pitch later. This is a concrete process detail — writing a future round's deck as a present-day planning tool — not generic "have a fundraising plan" advice.
quote: some of the best advice I got even when we were raising our series A as soon as we were done some of our investors when I was talking about it they said write your series B deck right now like if you believe you're going to do a series B you may never do it I mean we probably will but go write your series B deck

summary: To build Convoy's Series A hiring plan, Lewis drew a literal two-by-two grid mapping the credibility gaps he needed to close — demand-side marketplace expertise, supply-side marketplace expertise, and deep trucking-industry expertise — and wrote the actual name of every candidate he was recruiting that summer into the corresponding cell, while building the operations team in parallel so it would be in place for the September launch. The point wasn't just filling roles: he needed those specific hires visibly in progress so he could credibly tell Series A investors a story about de-risked execution capability. This is a reproducible artifact-level tactic (a literal grid with real candidate names on it), not generic "build a strong team" advice.
quote: I actually drew a grid it was like a two by two I was like I need to have people who understand you know demand side of the business in a marketplace the supply part of business in the marketplace really understand marketplace dynamics and really understand the trucking industry ... I took every person I was speaking with during my recruiting during the summer when we were building the product I was trying to get the operations team built in parallel so when we went to launch in September we'd have that already and we wrote everyone's name that we were recruiting on that sheet

summary: Convoy deliberately did not define company values at founding. In week one, Lewis and Grant took the first five engineers on a retreat to discuss who they wanted to be as a company, concluded "we don't know who we are yet," and consciously tabled the exercise. They returned to it roughly a year later, once the company had grown to about 30 people and Lewis could still talk to everyone end-to-end, extracting values (like "fall in love with problems not solutions" and "know why") from behaviors people already exhibited and liked, rather than inventing aspirational ones upfront. This runs against the common startup-culture advice to set values on day one — Lewis's position was that values can't be credibly articulated until there's enough lived organizational behavior to generalize from.
quote: we about a year into it we actually we first we first started the company the first five engineers and me and Grant went to like a retreat for a weekend this is week one just to kind of talk about who we wanted to be as a company and we started thinking about values and goals and we kind of said we don't know who we are yet right we paused that and then a year later we came back to it ... because we can see everybody like 30 people or whatever at that period I came around it was about 30 people

summary: Before co-founding Convoy, Lewis engineered a deliberate, multi-year career path specifically to earn technical credibility despite starting in management consulting rather than engineering. He describes roughly five or six sequential job moves — consulting to business development at a startup (which bought him startup credibility), to product management, to a role working directly with product engineering, to eventually managing engineering and product — each move calculated to close the gap toward a known end-state: enough credibility to be trusted by a technical co-founder and to be able to hire engineers himself. This is a specific, intentional career-sequencing strategy aimed at a predetermined future goal, not a generic "get more experience" narrative.
quote: I made a bunch of moves over different companies to work my way back towards the engineering side of the house quitting going to a startup where I was in business development but that gave me some startup credibility which allowed me to go into a product management role which let me move into like go to a startup where I could then work directly with product engineering which then let me go into a role where I managed engineering and product like five or six roles later I finally got to a place where I felt like I was in a place where I could now have credibility on the engineering side to go work with a co-founder who trusts me

summary: To validate the Convoy idea, Lewis ran a structured research process before ever talking to anyone in the industry: he researched the market and competitors online, then forced himself to write out every customer type and what each already pays money for (trucks, software, advice, etc.), combining these into several candidate business concepts written up like user stories. Only after narrowing to a few candidates did he go have field conversations with industry people, using those write-ups as a starting frame rather than going in fully open-ended. The process surfaced three genuinely distinct possible businesses within trucking, none matching his original desk-research hypotheses, and he picked one based on where people said they actually needed something rather than what he'd assumed going in.
quote: we kind of came up with combinations of those that seemed like the most interesting and then wrote actual like paragraph or two this is a summary of what that business would be so we will do this for this customer almost like a user story ... it didn't turn out to be any of the things we wrote down but there were actually three distinct businesses we came up with within trucking and then we picked one of those three and they were very different

summary: Lewis said working with investors hasn't been hard for him personally, but frames the relationship with a specific and somewhat counterintuitive warning: the moment they sign the first check is essentially the high point of the relationship, and it tends to get harder from there, not easier, once real difficulty hits. Because of that trajectory, he treats picking investors like dating, prioritizing genuine chemistry and the ability to have an open conversation during the easy early period, precisely because that same rapport gets tested later when things aren't going well. This runs counter to the assumption that investor relationships settle into smoother cooperation as trust builds over time — his experience is the reverse: the initial rapport is the peak, and everything after is harder until, maybe, a payoff moment much later.
quote: I haven't found it to be hard to work with investors but I think that I you've got to pick the right investors you have to like actually it's dating like when you meet the person you really want to actually enjoy the conversation you have with them and feel like there's some natural relationship and chemistry there because you're in the best of times when you're raising money from them for the first time right someday when it isn't so easy ... it only gets harder from the time they're signing that first check
